 {"id":"bd-14cc","title":"Workstream: Session UX Parity (in-app /resume /new /tree /fork)","description":"# Goal
Match legacy interactive session UX:
- `/resume` opens a picker inside the running app (not “restart with flags”).
- Session picker supports interactive delete.
- `/new` starts a new session without restarting the process.
- `/tree` provides full tree navigation UI + optional branch summarization.
- `/fork` creates a new session file from the current branch.

# Legacy Spec (Source-of-Truth)
## `/resume` and deleting sessions
- `legacy_pi_mono_code/pi-mono/packages/coding-agent/docs/session.md`:
  - sessions can be deleted interactively from `/resume` via `Ctrl+D` then confirm.
  - when available, pi uses the `trash` CLI to avoid permanent deletion.

## `/tree`
- `legacy_pi_mono_code/pi-mono/packages/coding-agent/docs/tree.md` defines:
  - interactive tree UI (depth-first navigation)
  - filters toggled by Ctrl+U (user only) and Ctrl+O (show all)
  - selection behavior differs for user vs non-user nodes
  - optional branch summarization with 3 options (none / default / custom prompt)
  - summary stored as branch_summary entry

## `/fork`
- `/fork` creates a **new session file** from the current branch (vs `/tree` which changes leaf within same session).

# Current Rust State (Gap)
- `src/interactive.rs` `/resume` and `/new` are placeholders that tell users to restart with flags.
- `/tree` exists but is not the full interactive navigator described in legacy docs.
- `/fork` behavior does not match the legacy “new session file” semantics.

# Scope / Deliverables
1) In-app `/resume` → show session picker UI → load selected session.
2) Session picker: delete sessions with Ctrl+D + confirmation (use `trash` if present).
3) `/new`: create a new session object (and file if persistence enabled), reset UI + agent context.
4) `/tree`: implement interactive tree navigator per legacy docs (filters, selection rules, summary prompt choices).
5) `/fork`: implement new-session-file fork semantics, with UX comparable to legacy.

# Testing
- Integration/state tests for:
  - /resume selection loads correct session
  - delete uses trash when available (mock command runner)
  - /new resets conversation + session metadata
  - /tree selection semantics and summary entry creation
  - /fork creates a new session file and switches

# Dependencies
- Session index integration improves performance but should not be required for correctness.
- Keybindings workstream (`bd-3ip`) is needed for consistent picker and tree controls.

## Acceptance Criteria
[ ] /resume works in-app (no restart)
[ ] Session picker supports delete with trash fallback
[ ] /new creates a fresh session without restart
[ ] /tree matches legacy navigation semantics
[ ] /fork creates a new session file and switches
[ ] Double-escape behavior matches settings

 {"id":"bd-14od","title":"Workstream: Rust API docs (rustdoc)","description":"# Goal
Make the public Rust API self-documenting via rustdoc so future refactors and downstream reuse are safer.

# Background
Even if the primary product is the `pi` binary, the repo exposes a library crate (`pi`) with many `pub` types. Today, many modules suppress doc-related clippy lints. That’s fine during rapid development, but we eventually want:
- accurate module-level docs
- docs for key public types (messages, sessions, providers, tools)
- minimal examples where it helps avoid misuse

# Scope
- Add/upgrade rustdoc comments for:
  - public structs/enums/traits that define the core contract
  - modules that are intended to be imported externally
- Ensure `cargo doc --no-deps` succeeds.

# Non-Goals
- Document every private helper.
- Build a separate "book"; this is rustdoc-first.

# Deliverables
- Improved rustdoc coverage across public API.
- A CI check (optional) to prevent regressions.","acceptance_criteria":"[ ] Scope in description implemented fully with no feature loss

 {"id":"bd-14sx","title":"E2E CLI: replace npm/git stubs with real local fixtures","description":"# Goal
Remove the npm/git stub scripts in `tests/e2e_cli.rs` by switching to real local fixtures and real git repos, while keeping tests fully offline and deterministic.

# Background
`tests/e2e_cli.rs` currently writes stub `npm` and `git` scripts into a temp PATH to simulate package manager flows. This is deterministic but still a fake. We want E2E coverage that exercises the real CLI flows using actual local packages and a real git repo (no network).

# Scope
- Replace npm stub with file-based/local package installs (tarball or `file:` spec) created in the test temp dir.
- Replace git stub with a real temporary git repo initialized in the harness temp dir; ensure deterministic commits and clean status.
- Keep tests offline (no network); ensure any external commands are deterministic and logged.
- Emit JSONL logs + artifact index (package files, git repo state, command transcripts).

# Files
- `tests/e2e_cli.rs`
- `tests/fixtures/**` (if new local packages are needed)

# Acceptance
- No stubbed npm/git scripts are used in these E2E tests.
- Package manager scenarios still pass offline and deterministically with logs + artifacts.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-05T06:27:46.530398790Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T02:37:09.143103183Z","closed_at":"2026-02-06T02:37:09.143034144Z","close_reason":"Completed: removed npm/git stubs in e2e_cli; use real offline npm file: fixtures + local git repos; add local git installed_path test","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-14sx","depends_on_id":"bd-c4q","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-07T03:28:01Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"bd-150s","title":"Phase 4: Execute Conformance - Official Pi-Mono Extensions (60)","description":"# Phase 4: Execute Conformance - Official Pi-Mono Extensions (60)

## Purpose
Run all 60 official pi-mono example extensions through the differential test runner. These are the MOST important extensions because they define the expected API surface. If any of these fail, we have a runtime bug.

## The 60 Official Extensions (by tier)

### Tier 1 - Minimal (14 extensions)
Single capability, no external deps:
hello, pirate, session-name, custom-footer, custom-header, system-prompt-header, preset, titlebar-spinner, status-line, model-status, widget-placement, question, qna, send-user-message

### Tier 2 - Events Only (11 extensions)
Subscribe to events, no tool registration:
auto-commit-on-exit, bash-spawn-hook, confirm-destructive, dirty-repo-guard, file-trigger, git-checkpoint, input-transform, protected-paths, trigger-compact, timed-confirm, permission-gate

### Tier 3 - Tools (7 extensions)
Register LLM-callable tools:
tools, truncated-tool, tool-override, inline-bash, ssh, interactive-shell, summarize

### Tier 4 - Multi-Capability (12 extensions)
Multiple registrations (tools + events + commands):
bookmark, claude-rules, custom-compaction, event-bus, handoff, notify, todo, mac-system-theme, shutdown-command, dynamic-resources (multi-file), rpc-demo

### Tier 5 - Multi-File (9 extensions)
Require multiple source files and/or npm deps:
doom-overlay, plan-mode, subagent, sandbox, with-deps, custom-provider-anthropic, custom-provider-gitlab-duo, custom-provider-qwen-cli, dynamic-resources

### Tier 6 - UI Heavy (7 extensions)
Complex UI: overlays, editors, games:
overlay-test, overlay-qa-tests, modal-editor, rainbow-editor, message-renderer, snake, space-invaders

## Execution Order
Run tiers in order (1 through 6). Each tier builds confidence before moving to more complex extensions. Early tiers catch fundamental issues; later tiers catch edge cases.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All 60 extensions attempted
- 100% pass rate for Tiers 1-3 (32 extensions)
- 95%+ pass rate for Tiers 4-5 (21 extensions)
- 80%+ pass rate for Tier 6 (7 extensions) -- UI extensions may have known differences
- All failures documented with root cause analysis","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:22:13.772092162Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-05T18:24:42.208949544Z","closed_at":"2026-02-05T17:54:48.963158102Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-150s","depends_on_id":"bd-3odv","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-07T03:28:12Z","created_by":"import"}],"comments":[{"id":3628,"issue_id":"bd-150s","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"EXECUTION STRATEGY: Run tiers in order. Tier 1 is the smoke test -- if hello.ts does not produce identical output in both runtimes, nothing else will work. Fix ALL Tier 1 failures before moving to Tier 2. Each tier exercises progressively more of the API surface, so failures at higher tiers likely indicate specific missing features rather than fundamental bugs.

TIER JUSTIFICATION:
- Tier 1 (14 exts): Basic loading, simple registrations. If these fail, the runtime is broken.
- Tier 2 (11 exts): Event system. Tests pi.on() and handler dispatch.
- Tier 3 (7 exts): Tool system. Tests registerTool() and execution.
- Tier 4 (12 exts): Multiple capabilities. Tests interaction between systems.
- Tier 5 (9 exts): Multi-file + deps. Tests module resolution and import handling.
- Tier 6 (7 exts): UI-heavy. Tests the most complex API surface (overlays, editors, games).","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:27:19Z"},{"id":3629,"issue_id":"bd-150s","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Ran CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/pi_agent_rust-target cargo test --test ext_conformance_diff. 27 passed, 2 failed.  {"id":"bd-19th","title":"E2E Interactive: /reload resources + autocomplete refresh","description":"# Goal
Add an interactive E2E script (tmux capture) proving `/reload` refreshes resources and autocomplete suggestions.

# Scope
- Start interactive session using the tmux harness from `bd-3hp`; capture screen frames as artifacts.
- Add/remove a skill or prompt template on disk, run `/reload`, and confirm autocomplete list updates.
- Verify diagnostics output for missing/invalid resources.

# Logging
- Record tmux capture files, stdout/stderr, and resource directory snapshots.
- Include step-by-step logs with timestamps.

# Acceptance Criteria
- Deterministic script with no network access.
- Artifacts sufficient to debug failures (captures + logs).

# Dependencies
- `bd-3hp` tmux capture harness.
- `/reload` parity (`bd-3nix`).
- Unified JSONL logging spec (`bd-4u9`).

 {"id":"bd-1a51i","title":"[TUI-3] Share command improvements: gist metadata, privacy choice, error handling","description":"## Problem

/share works but has rough edges: no gist title/description, privacy is hardcoded to private, error messages could be clearer.

## Solution

### Gist Metadata
- Use session name (if set) as gist description: `gh gist create --desc "Pi session: {name}"`
- Use timestamp if no name: `gh gist create --desc "Pi session 2026-02-13T03:15:00Z"`
- Filename: `pi-session-{name_or_timestamp}.html`

### Privacy Choice
- Default to private (current behavior)
- If user runs `/share public`, create public gist
- Show privacy in output: "Created private gist: https://..."

### Better Error Messages
- "gh not found" -> "Install GitHub CLI: brew install gh (macOS) or see https://cli.github.com"
- "gh not authenticated" -> "Run: gh auth login"
- Gist creation failure -> Show gh stderr output with context

### Copy URL to Clipboard
- After successful share, auto-copy viewer URL to clipboard
- **Existing infrastructure**: The project already has `arboard` (3.6.1) and `clipboard` (0.5.0) crates as optional dependencies, enabled by the `clipboard` feature (which is in the default feature set). The clipboard integration is already used for image pasting (`paste_image_from_clipboard()` at line ~963).
- Reuse the existing clipboard infrastructure:
  ```rust
  #[cfg(feature = "clipboard")]
  {
      if let Ok(mut ctx) = ClipboardContext::new() {
          let _ = ctx.set_contents(viewer_url.clone());
          // Show: "Viewer URL copied to clipboard"
      }
  }
  ```
- If clipboard feature is disabled, just skip the copy (no error)

## Files to Modify
- src/interactive.rs: /share handler (lines 10668-10813)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Gist gets descriptive title from session name or timestamp
- [ ] /share public creates public gist
- [ ] Default remains private
- [ ] Clear error messages with actionable recovery steps
- [ ] Clipboard copy using existing arboard/clipboard crate (feature-gated)
- [ ] Unit tests for metadata generation, privacy parsing, error message formatting","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:18:32.049110948Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T10:17:38.248635359Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T10:17:38.248606736Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

### What This Task Does
Track how much of the codebase each fuzz target exercises. Coverage data helps identify:
1. Which code paths are NOT being reached by fuzzing (need better seeds or strategies)
2. Which fuzz targets have diminishing returns (already saturated)
3. Overall fuzzing effectiveness trends over time

### Coverage Generation
cargo-fuzz supports coverage via:
```bash
cargo fuzz coverage <target>
# Generates coverage data in fuzz/coverage/<target>/
# Uses llvm-cov format
```

### Dashboard Options

**Option A: Local HTML report** (simpler)
```bash
cargo fuzz coverage fuzz_sse_parser
# Convert to HTML
llvm-cov show --format=html --output-dir=fuzz/coverage-report/ \
    target/x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/coverage/x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/release/fuzz_sse_parser
```

**Option B: CI-integrated coverage** (better long-term)
- Generate coverage in nightly CI job
- Upload as GitHub Pages artifact
- Track coverage percentage over time in a badge

### Key Metrics to Track
- Per-target: lines covered / total lines in relevant source files
- Per-target: branches covered / total branches
- Overall: unique code paths discovered per minute of fuzzing
- Trend: coverage growth rate (are we finding new paths or saturated?)

### Priority
This is P3 (nice-to-have) because fuzzing provides value even without coverage tracking. But coverage data helps prioritize which harnesses need more work.

### Files to Create/Modify
- Script: fuzz/generate-coverage.sh (runs coverage for all targets)
- Optional: .github/workflows/fuzz.yml (add coverage step to nightly job)

### Acceptance Criteria
- At least one target has a coverage report generated
- Coverage report shows which lines/branches are hit
- Documentation explains how to generate coverage locally","created_at":"2026-02-14T17:04:35Z"},{"id":2342,"issue_id":"bd-1akey","author":"BrightCat","text":"Completed takeover pass for FUZZ-P3.3: documented coverage dashboard workflow in fuzz/README.md, ensured fuzz/generate-coverage.sh is executable, and verified CI coverage dashboard job wiring in .github/workflows/fuzz.yml (line+branch coverage artifacts/markdown/json outputs). ## API Spec (from legacy)
```typescript
// Custom message (not for LLM, optional display)

  customType: string,
  content: string,
  display?: boolean,
  details?: unknown
}, {
  triggerTurn?: boolean,
  deliverAs?: 'steer' | 'followUp' | 'nextTurn'
});

// User message (triggers LLM turn)
pi.sendUserMessage(content, {
  deliverAs?: 'steer' | 'followUp'
});
```

## Delivery Modes
- **steer**: Interrupt after current tool, skip remaining tool calls
- **followUp**: Queue after agent finishes current turn
- **nextTurn**: Default, processed as next user input

## Implementation Requirements
1. Hostcall handlers for 'sendMessage' and 'sendUserMessage'
2. Integration with agent message queues (steering_queue, follow_up_queue)
3. Custom message type in session entries
4. Display logic in TUI for custom messages

## Data Flow
1. Extension calls pi.sendMessage({customType: 'bookmark', content: 'Saved\!'})
2. Hostcall creates CustomMessage entry
3. If display=true, shows in TUI
4. If triggerTurn=true, queues for processing

## Test Plan
- Unit test: sendMessage creates correct entry type
- Unit test: sendUserMessage triggers agent turn
- Integration test: steer mode interrupts current processing
- Integration test: followUp mode queues correctly

### Why This is P0 Priority
The SSE parser processes LIVE NETWORK DATA from LLM API responses. It's the first parser to touch untrusted bytes from the wire. Any crash here = denial of service during streaming.

### Harness Design

```rust
// fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_sse_parser.rs
#\![no_main]
use libfuzzer_sys::fuzz_target;

fuzz_target\!(|data: &[u8]| {
    // Test 1: Feed entire input as one chunk
    let mut parser = SseParser::new();
    let _ = parser.feed_into(data);
    let _ = parser.flush();
    
    // Test 2: Feed byte-by-byte (maximizes edge case exposure at chunk boundaries)
    let mut parser2 = SseParser::new();
    for byte in data {
        let _ = parser2.feed_into(&[*byte]);
    }
    let _ = parser2.flush();
    
    // Invariant: both should produce the same events
    // (This is the chunking invariant from the proptest, now with coverage guidance)
});
```

### Second Harness: SseStream (UTF-8 layer)

```rust
// fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_sse_stream.rs
#\![no_main]
use libfuzzer_sys::fuzz_target;

fuzz_target\!(|data: &[u8]| {
    // SseStream wraps SseParser and handles UTF-8 decoding
    let mut stream = SseStream::new();
    // Feed arbitrary bytes (may include invalid UTF-8)
    let _ = stream.feed(data);
    let _ = stream.flush();
});
```

### Seed Corpus
Extract SSE data from existing VCR cassettes and test fixtures:
- tests/fixtures/provider_responses/anthropic_stream.json → extract body_chunks
- tests/fixtures/provider_responses/openai_stream.json → extract body_chunks
- tests/fixtures/provider_responses/gemini_stream.json → extract body_chunks
- Generate edge case seeds: BOM-prefixed, bare CR, empty events, oversized data fields

### Coverage Goals
- feed_into(): All branches for CRLF, LF, CR line endings
- process_line(): All field types (data, event, id, retry, comment, unknown)
- flush(): Incomplete buffer handling
- SseStream UTF-8: Incomplete multibyte sequences, replacement chars

### Expected Findings
Based on code review, likely bugs:
- Memory exhaustion via unbounded data field accumulation (no max size limit)
- Potential issues with bare CR at buffer boundary (split across chunks)
- UTF-8 decoder behavior on intentionally malformed sequences

### Files to Create
- fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_sse_parser.rs
- fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_sse_stream.rs
- fuzz/corpus/fuzz_sse_parser/ (seed files)

### Run Command
```bash
cargo fuzz run fuzz_sse_parser -- -max_total_time=300  # 5 minutes
cargo fuzz run fuzz_sse_stream -- -max_total_time=300
```

### Acceptance Criteria
- Both harnesses compile and run without issues
- Seed corpus includes at least 10 files from real provider responses
- 5-minute fuzz run completes without crashes
- Any crashes found are triaged, minimized (cargo fuzz tmin), and fixed","created_at":"2026-02-14T16:59:56Z"},{"id":2785,"issue_id":"bd-5h0cd","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Already implemented. fuzz/fuzz_targets/ contains fuzz_sse_parser.rs (2.6KB) and fuzz_sse_stream.rs (3.2KB).  {"id":"bd-anewk","title":"[PERF-4] Frame budget enforcement with degraded rendering modes","description":"## Problem

When the system is under load, the TUI continues trying to render at full fidelity 60fps, which can make the terminal unresponsive. Need automatic degradation.

## Solution: Frame Budget State Machine

### CRITICAL DESIGN: view() is &self — Interior Mutability Required

The charmed_bubbletea Model trait defines `fn view(&self) -> String`. The FrameBudget state machine transitions happen based on frame timing measured INSIDE view(). Since view() only has &self, all mutable FrameBudget fields must use Cell<T>.

Architecture:
- **FrameBudget** (Cell<T> fields) — frame-level state, updated in view(&self)
- **cpu_pressure** (plain PiApp field) — system-level state, updated in update(&mut self) via tick

```rust
#[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq)]
enum RenderFidelity { Normal, Degraded, Emergency }

/// Frame-level budget tracking. Uses Cell<T> because state transitions
/// occur inside view(&self) after measuring frame render time.
/// All fields are Copy types, so Cell (not RefCell) suffices.
struct FrameBudget {
    fidelity: Cell<RenderFidelity>,
    consecutive_over: Cell<u8>,   // frames exceeding budget
    consecutive_under: Cell<u8>,  // frames under budget (for recovery)
}

impl FrameBudget {
    /// Called at END of view() with the frame's render time.
    /// Updates fidelity for the NEXT frame (one-frame delay is imperceptible).
    fn record_frame(&self, frame_time: Duration) {
        let budget_us = match self.fidelity.get() {
            RenderFidelity::Normal => 16_000,    // 60fps
            RenderFidelity::Degraded => 33_000,  // 30fps
            RenderFidelity::Emergency => 66_000, // 15fps
        };
        if frame_time.as_micros() as u64 > budget_us {
            self.consecutive_under.set(0);
            let over = self.consecutive_over.get() + 1;
            self.consecutive_over.set(over);
            if over >= 3 {
                self.consecutive_over.set(0);
                let new_fidelity = match self.fidelity.get() {
                    RenderFidelity::Normal => RenderFidelity::Degraded,
                    RenderFidelity::Degraded => RenderFidelity::Emergency,
                    RenderFidelity::Emergency => RenderFidelity::Emergency,
                };
                self.fidelity.set(new_fidelity);
            }
        } else {
            self.consecutive_over.set(0);
            let under = self.consecutive_under.get() + 1;
            self.consecutive_under.set(under);
            if under >= 10 {
                self.consecutive_under.set(0);
                // Recovery with hysteresis (25% margin)
                let recovered = match self.fidelity.get() {
                    RenderFidelity::Emergency => RenderFidelity::Degraded,
                    RenderFidelity::Degraded => RenderFidelity::Normal,
                    RenderFidelity::Normal => RenderFidelity::Normal,
                };
                self.fidelity.set(recovered);
            }
        }
    }
}
```

Note: budget_us is DERIVED from fidelity, not a stored field. Fewer fields = fewer Cell operations = clearer code.

### CPU Pressure: Separate from FrameBudget

CPU pressure is a system-wide signal that changes slowly (load averages are 1-minute exponential moving averages). It is sampled in update(&mut self) via a periodic tick, NOT inside view().

```rust
// In PiApp (plain fields, updated in update(&mut self)):
cpu_pressure: PressureLevel,
cpu_pressure_last_sample: Instant,

// In update(&mut self, msg):
fn update(&mut self, msg: PiMsg) -> Cmd {
    // On every message, check if 5 seconds have elapsed
    if self.cpu_pressure_last_sample.elapsed() > Duration::from_secs(5) {
        self.cpu_pressure = sample_load_average(self.cpu_count);
        self.cpu_pressure_last_sample = Instant::now();
    }
    // ...
}

// In view(&self):
fn view(&self) -> String {
    // Combine frame budget with CPU pressure floor
    let frame_fidelity = self.frame_budget.fidelity.get();
    let effective = min(frame_fidelity, self.cpu_pressure.to_fidelity_floor());
    // Use effective fidelity for rendering decisions
    // ...
}
```

Platform-specific load average reading:
```rust
fn sample_load_average(cpu_count: usize) -> PressureLevel {
    let load = read_load_avg();  // returns Option<f64>
    match load {
        None => PressureLevel::Normal, // Non-Linux/macOS: always Normal
        Some(load) => {
            let ratio = load / cpu_count as f64;
            if ratio < 1.5 { PressureLevel::Normal }
            else if ratio < 3.0 { PressureLevel::Moderate }
            else { PressureLevel::High }
        }
    }
}

fn read_load_avg() -> Option<f64> {
    #[cfg(target_os = "linux")]
    {
        let content = std::fs::read_to_string("/proc/loadavg").ok()?;
        content.split_whitespace().next()?.parse().ok()
    }
    #[cfg(any(target_os = "macos", target_os = "freebsd"))]
    {
        let mut loadavg = [0.0f64; 1];
        let ret = unsafe { libc::getloadavg(loadavg.as_mut_ptr(), 1) };
        if ret == 1 { Some(loadavg[0]) } else { None }
    }
    #[cfg(not(any(target_os = "linux", target_os = "macos", target_os = "freebsd")))]
    { None }
}
```

### State Machine

```
Normal (60fps, full markdown)
  | 3 consecutive frames > 16ms
  v
Degraded (30fps, simplified markdown)
  | 3 consecutive frames > 33ms
  v
Emergency (15fps, raw text, no markdown)
  ^ 10 consecutive frames < budget (with 25% hysteresis margin)
```

### Degradation Levels

1. **Normal**: Full markdown rendering via glamour::render_with_word_wrap(), 60fps, all visual features
2. **Degraded**: Lightweight markdown (textwrap::fill() with bold/italic only, no syntax highlighting), effective 30fps via frame skipping (skip even-numbered frames, return cached view output)
3. **Emergency**: Raw text (no markdown at all), effective 15fps (skip 3 of 4 frames). Skip thinking blocks, minimal chrome.

### Frame Skipping Implementation
```rust
// In view(&self):
let frame_count = self.frame_timing.total_frames.get();
let fidelity = effective_fidelity;
let should_skip = match fidelity {
    RenderFidelity::Normal => false,
    RenderFidelity::Degraded => frame_count % 2 != 0,
    RenderFidelity::Emergency => frame_count % 4 != 0,
};
if should_skip {
    // Return cached last output (stored in RefCell<String>)
    return self.cached_view_output.borrow().clone();
}
```

- src/interactive.rs: FrameBudget struct (Cell<T> fields), cpu_pressure PiApp fields, integration into view()/update()

## Dependencies
- Depends on PERF-3 (frame timing telemetry -- need Instant::now() and total_frames counter)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] FrameBudget with Cell<RenderFidelity>, Cell<u8> for counters
- [ ] cpu_pressure as plain PiApp field, sampled in update() via elapsed check
- [ ] Effective fidelity = min(frame_budget, cpu_pressure_floor)
- [ ] 3 consecutive over-budget frames trigger degradation
- [ ] 10 consecutive under-budget frames trigger recovery (with 25% hysteresis)
- [ ] Degraded mode: textwrap-only markdown, frame skipping (effective 30fps)
- [ ] Emergency mode: raw text, frame skipping (effective 15fps)
- [ ] Platform-specific load average: /proc/loadavg (Linux), libc::getloadavg (macOS), no-op (other)
- [ ] cached_view_output in RefCell<String> for frame skipping

## Unit Tests (in tests/tui_state.rs)
- `tui_perf_frame_budget_normal_to_degraded` -- 3 slow frames triggers Degraded
- `tui_perf_frame_budget_degraded_to_emergency` -- sustained slow triggers Emergency
- `tui_perf_frame_budget_recovery` -- 10 fast frames triggers recovery to Normal
- `tui_perf_frame_budget_hysteresis` -- borderline frames dont oscillate
- `tui_perf_frame_budget_cell_works_in_ref` -- verify Cell<T> works through &self reference
- `tui_perf_cpu_pressure_level_calculation` -- load/cpu_count mapped correctly
- `tui_perf_effective_fidelity_combines_budget_and_pressure` -- min() logic correct

   - write to `~/.pi/agent/settings.json` (global) and/or `.pi/settings.json` (project)
   - follow existing merge semantics from `src/config.rs`
3) Ensure changes take effect without restart where possible.

# Notes
- Avoid editing raw JSON in the TUI for now; prefer focused selectors.
- For settings that depend on other workstreams (themes, keybindings), include partial functionality but keep UX consistent.

# Testing
- Unit tests for persistence and merge semantics.
- State tests for `/settings` selection flows.

# Dependencies
- Keybinding workstream (`bd-3ip`) for consistent navigation.
- Theme system workstream (`bd-22p`) for actual theme switching.
